coredata.dc:title: Construction of cerium oxide nanoparticles immobilized on the surface of zinc vanadate nanoflowers for accelerated photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline under visible light irradiation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Chen, Jiayi
Chen, Xiaoting
Ning, Xiaomei
Zhan, Liang
Zhou, Xiaosong
coredata.dc:description: Construction of Z-scheme heterojunction has been deemed to be an effective and promising approach to boost the photocatalytic activity on account of accelerating the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers and maintaining the strong redox ability. Herein, an attractive CeO2/Zn3V2O8 Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst was rationally constructed by zero-dimensional (0D) CeO2 nanoparticles immobilized on the surface of three-dimensional (3D) Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers using a simple mixing method, and applied to the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. As expected, it was observed that the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid illustrated significantly boosted the photocatalytic activity for the elimination of TC compared to pure Zn3V2O8. More importantly, the optimized CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid owned the largest elimination rate of TC with 1.13 × 10−2 min−1, which was around 8.1 and 3.8 times as high as single CeO2 (0.14 × 10−2 min−1) and Zn3V2O8 (0.30 × 10−2 min−1), respectively. The appreciable performance improvement was mainly ascribed to the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8, facilitating the transfer rate of photogenerated carriers and remaining the high reducibility of photoexcited electrons in CeO2 and strong oxidizability of photoinduced holes in Zn3V2O8. Active species capture experiments and electron spin resonance spectra showed that superoxide radicals and holes were the main active species for TC degradation. Besides, the possible degradation pathways of TC were speculated by identifying degradation intermediates, and the reasonable degradation mechanism including migration and transport behaviors of charge carriers and generation processes of reactive species were revealed in depth. This investigation enriches Zn3V2O8-based Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalytic system and offers a new inspiration for the construction and fabrication of high-efficiency Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts to remove the antibiotics from wastewater.

head.ce:title: Construction of cerium oxide nanoparticles immobilized on the surface of zinc vanadate nanoflowers for accelerated photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline under visible light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Research Center for Clean Energy Materials Chemical Engineering Technology of Guangdong, Key Laboratory of Clean Energy Materials Chemistry of Guangdong Higher Education Institutes, Lingnan Normal University, Zhanjiang 524048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Research Center for Clean Energy Materials Chemical Engineering Technology of Guangdong
Key Laboratory of Clean Energy Materials Chemistry of Guangdong Higher Education Institutes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Research Center for Clean Energy Materials Chemical Engineering Technology of Guangdong, Key Laboratory of Clean Energy Materials Chemistry of Guangdong Higher Education Institutes, Lingnan Normal University, Zhanjiang 524048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CeO2 nanoparticles were tightly immobilized on the surface of Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers to construct the unique 0D/3D “point-to-body” CeO2/Zn3V2O8 Z-scheme heterojunction hybrid, efficiently facilitating the charge transfer and separation for the photocatalytic degradation of TC under visible light irradiation.

Construction of Z-scheme heterojunction has been deemed to be an effective and promising approach to boost the photocatalytic activity on account of accelerating the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers and maintaining the strong redox ability. Herein, an attractive CeO2/Zn3V2O8 Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst was rationally constructed by zero-dimensional (0D) CeO2 nanoparticles immobilized on the surface of three-dimensional (3D) Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers using a simple mixing method, and applied to the photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. As expected, it was observed that the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid illustrated significantly boosted the photocatalytic activity for the elimination of TC compared to pure Zn3V2O8. More importantly, the optimized CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid owned the largest elimination rate of TC with 1.13 × 10−2 min−1, which was around 8.1 and 3.8 times as high as single CeO2 (0.14 × 10−2 min−1) and Zn3V2O8 (0.30 × 10−2 min−1), respectively. The appreciable performance improvement was mainly ascribed to the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8, facilitating the transfer rate of photogenerated carriers and remaining the high reducibility of photoexcited electrons in CeO2 and strong oxidizability of photoinduced holes in Zn3V2O8. Active species capture experiments and electron spin resonance spectra showed that superoxide radicals and holes were the main active species for TC degradation. Besides, the possible degradation pathways of TC were speculated by identifying degradation intermediates, and the reasonable degradation mechanism including migration and transport behaviors of charge carriers and generation processes of reactive species were revealed in depth. This investigation enriches Zn3V2O8-based Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalytic system and offers a new inspiration for the construction and fabrication of high-efficiency Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts to remove the antibiotics from wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, multitudinous antibiotics have been intensively employed to the treatment of human and animal diseases owing to their prominent antibacterial effect and limited side effect [1]. Unfortunately, more and more antibiotics have been detected in the aquatic environment such as rivers and lakes due to the widespread and excessive utilization and pharmaceutical wastewater discharge [2]. More frustratingly, numerous antibiotics are directly discharged into the natural environment since they are high chemical stability and very hard to thoroughly disintegrated, resulting in a marked threat to human health and ecosystem equilibrium [3]. To this end, diverse techniques have been explored for removing these antibiotics from aquatic environment, such as absorption, flocculation, membrane separation, filtration, biodegradation, and many more [4–7]. Among them, benefiting from its environmental friendliness, low cost, high efficiency and strong mineralization ability, photocatalytic purification technology has been considered to be one of the most effective, promising and sustainable approaches to eliminate antibiotics from wastewater, because it can entirely decompose the antibiotics into CO2 and H2O under solar irradiation without causing secondary pollution [3,5,8,9]. Hitherto, multitudinous photocatalysts including TiO2-based photocatalysts [10], metal-organic frameworks-based photocatalysts [11], AgI-based photocatalysts [12], graphene-based photocatalysts [13], ZnO-based photocatalysts [14], CdS-based photocatalysts [15], layered double hydroxides-based photocatalysts [16], Bi2MoO6-based photocatalysts [17], graphitic carbon nitride-based photocatalysts [18], and so forth, have been explored for the degradation of organic pollutions under visible light irradiation. Among them, zinc vanadate (Zn3V2O8) has attracted tremendous concern as a promising visible-light-induced photocatalyst for the elimination of refractory pollutants from wastewater due to its nontoxicity, low cost, moderate band gap, promising charge mobility, unique hexagonal structure, positive valence band potential with strong oxidizability (ca. 3.0 eV) and high chemical stability [19–21]. Frustratingly, the photocatalytic activity of pure Zn3V2O8 is not satisfactory owing to the limited visible light harvesting and fast recombination of photogenerated carriers [19,20].

Luckily, the construction of heterojunction has been considered to be an effective way for improving the photocatalytic activity originated from boosting the light response range and accelerating the migration rate of photoexcited carriers [19,20]. In general, the charge migration pathway of heterojunction photocatalyst follows either traditional type II heterojunction or Z-scheme heterojunction [22]. Unfortunately, the reducibility of photogenerated electrons and oxidizability of photogenerated holes were ineluctably reduced after charge migration in the conventional type-II heterojunction [23]. On the contrary, the Z-scheme heterojunction could solve this problem because it could adjust the transmission channel of photogenerated carriers in heterojunction, so that retaining the strong redox ability [24]. As a consequence, the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction was more superiority. Nevertheless, there were just a few reported literatures in regard to Z-scheme photocatalysts containing Zn3V2O8 so far.

Fortunately, cerium oxide (CeO2), as an abundant rare metal oxides on earth, has been explored as a promising visible light responsive photocatalyst on account of its special electron orbital structure, appropriate band gap, rich oxygen vacancies, negative conduction band potential with strong reducibility (ca. −0.40 eV), nontoxicity and low cost [25–27]. More importantly, it was found that the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) values of Zn3V2O8 were at around −0.01 and 2.92 eV, and the CB and VB of CeO2 were at approximately −0.40 and 2.50 eV, respectively [19,28]. Considering the above advantages of CeO2 and well-matched band positions between Zn3V2O8 and CeO2, it was likely to form the Z-scheme heterojunction in theory between Zn3V2O8 and CeO2. Once the Z-scheme heterojunction between Zn3V2O8 and CeO2 was formed in nanoscale, the photoexcited electrons in the CB of Zn3V2O8 transfer to the VB of CeO2 and fast recombine with each other, resulting in the photogenerated electrons in the CB of CeO2 possessed high reducibility and the photoexcited holes in the VB of Zn3V2O8 owned strong oxidizability. In this way, it was expected that the recombination of photoexcited carrier was significantly restrain and the high redox ability was well maintained at the same time.

Inspired by the above investigations, herein, a promising CeO2/Zn3V2O8 Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst composed of zero-dimensional (0D) CeO2 nanoparticles decorated onto the surface of three-dimensional (3D) Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers was successfully constructed through a simple mixing approach. It was noteworthy that 3D Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers consisted of several pieces of Zn3V2O8 nanosheets could provide a large exposed surface for the loading of 0D CeO2 nanoparticles, which was beneficial to numerous CeO2 nanoparticles were well immobilized on the surface of Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers, promoting the formation of interface heterojunctions between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 and accelerating the charge separation. Tetracycline (TC) as a refractory antibiotic was employed to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid under visible light irradiation. Multitudinous characterization technologies were employed to perform the structure, composition and photoelectrical properties of resulting CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid. As expected, compared to pure Zn3V2O8, the as-synthesized CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid exhibited significantly boosted photocatalytic performance towards TC degradation under visible light irradiation, which was mainly attributed to the highly efficient Z-scheme charge separation. The dominant active species were identified by active species capture experiments and electron spin resonance spectra. Additionally, a possible pathway for TC degradation by CeO2/Zn3V2O8 and a rational degradation mechanism were proposed. It is expected that this work provides a new inspiration for designing highly efficient Zn3V2O8-based Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts for energy conversion and environmental remediation.
This section was described in Supporting Information.

The crystal phase of the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid was determined by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Fig. 1 showed that pure Zn3V2O8 owned ten dominating diffraction peaks at 2θ = 18.68°, 26.41°, 27.04°, 29.44°, 30.96°, 34.88°, 35.96°, 43.12°, 57.71° and 62.93° were in agreement with the (120), (220), (211), (131), (040), (122), (320), (042), (162) and (442) planes of the orthorhombic phase Zn3V2O8 (JCPDS No. 34-0378), respectively [19,20]. The typical diffraction peaks of bare CeO2 at 2θ = 28.72°, 33.24°, 47.72° and 56.47° were indexed as the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of the cubic phase CeO2 (JCPDS No. 34-0394), respectively [26,29]. Noticeably, the typical diffraction peaks of Zn3V2O8 and CeO2 were clearly observed in the XRD spectra of the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 composites, implying that the CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid was constructed successfully. Interestingly, it was found that the intensities of typical diffraction peaks for Zn3V2O8 weakened step by step, while the intensities of main diffraction peaks for CeO2 gradually increased with the increasing content of CeO2, probably implying that there existed a close interaction between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 in prepared composite. Besides, there were no other impurity peaks were observed in Fig. 1, suggesting that all the prepared samples owned good crystallinity and purity.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was employed to identify the element composition and valence state of the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid, and elucidated the interaction between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 in prepared composite. Fig. S1 displayed that the XPS survey spectra of Zn3V2O8 and CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 were composed of Zn, V, O and Ce, Zn, V, O elements, respectively, manifesting that CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 coexisted in the prepared composite. Fig. 2 a presented the high resolution Ce 3d spectrum of CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8, it was found that there were eight main peaks at binding energies of 882.33, 884.28, 888.87, 898.32, 900.85, 902.60, 907.38 and 916.69 eV. The peaks centered at 882.33, 884.28, 888.87 and 898.32 eV were assigned to Ce 3d5/2, and the peaks at 900.85, 902.60, 907.38 and 916.69 eV could be attributed to Ce 3d3/2, proving that the existence of Ce4+ in the composite [30,31]. From Fig. 2b, it was observed that the Zn 2p spectrum of pure Zn3V2O8 owned two characteristic peaks at 1021.45 and 1044.57 eV, which could be attributed to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively, manifesting that the existence of Zn2+ in Zn3V2O8 [20,32]. As presented in Fig. 2c, the V 2p spectrum of bare Zn3V2O8 showed two obvious peaks at 516.81 and 524.16 eV, which could be ascribed to V 2p5/2 and V 2p3/2, respectively, indicating that V existed in the form of V5+ in Zn3V2O8 [20,21]. For the O 1 s spectrum of pristine Zn3V2O8 (Fig. 2d), it was found that the two peaks at binding energies of 529.85 and 531.28 eV belonged to the lattice oxygen and the surface adsorbed oxygen [30,31]. Interestingly, compared to pure Zn3V2O8, it was observed that the characteristic peak positions of Zn 2p, V 2p and O 1 s for CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 had slightly positive shift (ca. 0.3 eV), suggesting that the chemical environment of Zn, V and O had changed after Zn3V2O8 and CeO2 combined to form the CeO2/Zn3V2O8 heterojunction, which was probably due to the existence of the strong interaction between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 [30].

In order to further get an insight into the existence of interaction between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 in as-obtained composite, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum measurement was carried out. As illustrated in Fig. S2, it was observed that the FT-IR spectrum of single Zn3V2O8 depicted the two characteristic peaks at 842 and 663 cm−1, which could be attributed to the vibration modes of tetrahedral VO4, and the peaks at 794 and 515 cm−1 were ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric vibration modes of VOZn and VOV, respectively [19,33]. Besides, the peak at around 418 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of ZnO band [19]. Interestingly, it was found that there were two broad and weak peaks at 3452 and 1628 cm−1, which were respectively belonged to the bending and stretching vibration of HOH [19,34]. In addition, as for pristine CeO2, there were two weak peaks at 1385 and 401 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of CeOCe and the symmetric stretching vibration of CeO, respectively [35,36]. Noticeably, all the synthesized CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrids owned the typical peaks of both CeO2 and Zn3V2O8, corroborating that the CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid was successfully constructed. More interestingly, compared to pure Zn3V2O8, it was clear to see that the peak at 418 cm−1 slightly moved to the lower wavenumber, and the peak at 515 cm−1 gradually disappeared in the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid with the increase content of CeO2, which was probably due to the interfacial interaction between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8. It was well consistent with the aforesaid XPS analysis.

The morphologies and microstructures of pure Zn3V2O8, CeO2 and CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As could be seen from Fig. 3 a and b, pure Zn3V2O8 exhibited the three-dimensional (3D) hierarchical flower-like morphology, which was comprised of numerous interconnected nanosheets. From Fig. 3c, we could see that single CeO2 revealed the zero-dimensional (0D) sphere-like morphology assembled with multitudinous irregular and agglomerate nanoparticles. As expected, it could be seen in Fig. 3d and e that multiple 0D CeO2 nanoparticles tightly immobilized on the surface of 3D Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers. Fig. 3f further elucidated that numerous CeO2 nanoparticles were well attached to the surface of Zn3V2O8. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 3g, it was clear to see that there were two types of lattice fringes with d-spacing were measured to be about 0.31 and 0.255 nm, corresponding to the (110) face of CeO2 and (122) plane of Zn3V2O8, respectively [21,31], suggesting that the presence of a heterojunction between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8, which was beneficial to promote the charge migration at the tight heterojunction interface. Besides, the energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) analysis was further employed to detected the elemental compositions of CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8. As exhibited in Fig. 3h, it was clear to see that the elements of Ce, Zn, V and O were detected in the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid, and the corresponding elemental mappings in Fig. S3 showed that the elements of Ce, Zn, V and O were uniformly dispersed in the whole observed region, further explaining the unique 0D/3D CeO2/Zn3V2O8 heterojunction hybrid was successfully constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activities of the resulting photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: In this investigation, TC as a typical refractory antibiotic was employed to assess the photocatalytic performances of the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrids under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 400 nm). As plotted in Fig. S4, it was observed that the concentration of TC kept constant after stirring in dark for 60 min, suggesting that 60 min was sufficient to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between photocatalyst and TC. Besides, under dark conditions for 1 h, the TC adsorption rates by pure CeO2, Zn3V2O8, CeO2(10 wt%)/Zn3V2O8, CeO2(20 wt%)/Zn3V2O8, CeO2(30 wt%)/Zn3V2O8, CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 and CeO2(50 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 were about 55.7%, 33.5%, 36.9%, 36.6%, 40.6%, 38.3% and 39.8%, respectively. Notably, pure CeO2 displayed the largest adsorption capacity of TC, which was probably due to the larger specific surface area. Interestingly, the adsorption capacity of TC for Zn3V2O8 was slightly increased after the introduction of CeO2. As presented in Fig. 4 a, the blank experiment demonstrated that TC was hardly degraded in the absence of photocatalyst, implying that TC was stable and difficult to self-degradation under visible light irradiation. After 70 min of visible light irradiation, only 19.6% and 9.9% of TC were removed in presence of pure Zn3V2O8 and CeO2, respectively, which was attributed to the rapid recombination of photoinduced charge carriers. Noticeably, it was clear to see that the photocatalytic activities of all the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrids were much higher than that of pristine Zn3V2O8 and CeO2, and the degradation efficiency of TC increased first and then decreased with the increase in the content of CeO2. The CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid owned the largest photocatalytic performance of TC, and the removal rate of TC was up to 53.4% after irradiated by visible light for 70 min, which was approximately 5.4 and 2.7 times as high as pure CeO2 and Zn3V2O8, respectively. The enhancement was mainly ascribed to the successful construction of unique 0D/3D CeO2/Zn3V2O8 Z-scheme heterojunction, facilitating the separation and transfer of photoinduced carriers. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic activity of CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 was still unsatisfactory, so we prolonged the visible light irradiation time to boost its degradation efficiency of TC. It could be seen from Fig. S5, about 96% of TC could be degraded by the CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 composite under visible light irradiation for 140 min, demonstrating that the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid could make full use of visible light to remove the refractory antibiotics from wastewater. More frustratingly, it was observed that the photocatalytic performance of the CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid was not continue to increase with an increase in the amount of CeO2, and the reasons might be as follows, firstly, when the content of CeO2 was relatively low, the limited contact areas between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 could not construct adequately Z-scheme heterojunctions and accomplish rapid charge transfer [37]. Secondly, when moderate CeO2 nanoparticles were immobilized on the surface of Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers, the synergistic effect between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 could construct sufficiently Z-scheme heterojunctions and promote the migration of photoexcited carriers [38]. Thirdly, when the content of CeO2 was relatively high, the synergistic effect between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 would be hindered due to the excessive CeO2 nanoparticles on the surface of Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers agglomerated or acted as the recombination center of photoexcited carriers, resulting in the inferior photocatalytic activity [25,26]. Besides, from Fig. 4b, it was observed that the intensity of the characteristic absorption peak for TC at around 357 nm gradually decreased as the increase of exposure time, elucidating that the structure of TC was successfully destroyed by the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid under the visible light irradiation. Besides, total organic carbon (TOC) analysis was employed to evaluate the mineralization ability of the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid. As could be seen from Fig. S6, it was observed that the mineralization rate of TC achieved to 33.9% over the CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid after 80 min irradiation with visible light, suggesting that the refractory TC antibiotic could be effectively mineralized by the obtained CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid under visible light irradiation.

The reusability of the CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid was appraised by implementing the recyclability experiment for the photocatalytic degradation of TC under visible light irradiation. After the photocatalytic reaction, the CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid was removed directly from the liquid by centrifugation and washed with distilled water and absolute alcohol for several times for the next cycle. From Fig. 5 a, it was observed that the photocatalytic activity of the CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid showed no significant deterioration after four successive cycles, proving that the resulting CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid possessed the favorable reproducibility for TC degradation. Besides, as could be seen from Fig. 5b and Fig. S7, there were no unambiguous variations in the XRD and FTIR patterns of fresh and recycled CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8, confirming the good stability and durability of the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid.

Furthermore, the effects of pH, TC concentration and dosage of photocatalyst on the photocatalytic performance were further investigated and the results were shown in Fig. S8. From Fig. S8a, it was clear to see that the degradation efficiency of TC was rapidly increased from 23.2% to 53.4% with the dosage of photocatalyst increasing from 25 mg to 50 mg, which was attributed to the presence of more exposed reactive sites [42]. Interestingly, it was observed that the removal rate of TC improved not so much when the dosage of photocatalyst further increased to 100 mg, which was probably due to the excess photocatalyst hindered the transmission of light [1,6]. Moreover, it was discovered that the removal rate of TC decreased gradually with increasing initial concentration of TC (Fig. S8b), indicating that TC concentration had a certain inhibition effect. This phenomenon might be owing to the intermediates would increase with the increasing of the initial concentration of TC, which would lead to more competition between TC molecules and intermediates for the limited reactive sites of photocatalysts [43,44]. As depicted in Fig. S8c, it was found that the removal efficiency of TC was rapidly increased from 39.7% to 53.4% with the value of pH increasing from 2.98 to 6.86, which was attributed to the acid corrosion of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 during the strong acidic pH [45]. Interestingly, it was observed that the removal rate of TC improved not so much when the value of pH further increased to 8.51, which was probably ascribed to the deprotonation of TC molecules and the change of photocatalyst surface charge [45,46].

, it is of great significance to investigate the effects of coexisting inorganic ions on the photocatalytic performance of the CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid. From Fig. 6 a, it was observed that Ca2+ possessed a minor inhabitation effect, while Na+, K+ and Mg2+ had significant inhibitory effect for the elimination of TC in the presence of CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8, which was probably ascribed to the fact that Na+, K+ and Mg2+ combined with TC to generate TC-metal complexes or there existed the competence adsorption between the cationic and TC [38,47]. SO 4
2 -
acted as radical scavengers could react with hydroxyl radicals [38,48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of CeO2/Zn3V2O8 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: To definitely probe the boosted photocatalytic performance of the CeO2/Zn3V2O8 Z-scheme photocatalyst, multitudinous characterizations were employed to elucidate the diverse physicochemical features including specific surface area (SSA), light absorption property, band gap structure and charge separation efficiency. Firstly, nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the synthesized samples were measured, and the corresponding results were presented in Fig. 7 a and Table 1 . From Fig. 7a, it was observed that all samples depicted type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, suggesting that they owned the mesoporous structures [49], and the corresponding pore diameter distributions (Fig. S9) were further substantiated. As could be seen from Table 1, pure CeO2 possessed the largest SSA, but it owned the lowest elimination rate of TC. Interestingly, the generated CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid owned the highest removal rate of TC, but its SSA was moderate. Moreover, to further gain an insight into the intrinsic photocatalytic activity of the generated CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid, the apparent rate constant (k) was ingeniously normalized to the SSA, referred to ks , and the corresponding results were also listed in Table 1. Observably, it was discovered that the ks value of CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 was calculated to be 9.6 × 10−4 min−1·L·m−2, which was 16.0 and 1.7 times larger than that of bare CeO2 (0.6 × 10−4 min−1·L·m−2) and Zn3V2O8 (5.7 × 10−4 min−1·L·m−2), respectively. As a result, it was observed that there was no obvious relationship between the removal rate of TC and SSA, suggesting that SSA was not the most important reason for the boosted photocatalytic activity.

The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was employed to elucidate the light absorption properties of the prepared CeO2, Zn3V2O8 and CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrids. As could be seen in Fig. 7b, the characteristic absorption edges of Zn3V2O8 and CeO2 were observed at about 410 and 430 nm, respectively. Importantly, after the introduction of CeO2, the as-prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrids presented obviously enhanced visible-light absorption in comparison to that of single Zn3V2O8, which was attributed to the synergistic effect between Zn3V2O8 and CeO2, especially the photosensitizing effect of CeO2 [36]. This enhancement was helpful for the utilization of the sunlight for the obtained CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrids. Hence, these findings clearly illustrated that the incorporation of CeO2 boosted the visible light utilization of Zn3V2O8, and the superior visible-light driven photocatalytic performance would be appeared. Frustratingly, it was observed that there was no straightforward relationship between visible light absorption and the degradation efficiency of TC, suggesting that the light absorption ability was not the pivotal factor in the boosted photocatalytic activity.

Additionally, the band gap energies (Eg ) of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were calculated by using the Kubelka-Munk function [5,50–52]: αhv = A(hv − Eg)n/2 . According to the previous literatures [19,53], both n values of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were 1. As could be seen from Fig. 7c and 7d, the Eg of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 could be extrapolated to be 2.94 and 3.00 eV according to the (αhʋ)2 against (hʋ) curves, respectively. On the other side, the conduction band (ECB ) and valence band (EVB ) values of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were preliminarily calculated by the following empirical formulas [54–56]: EVB = X − Ec + 0.5Eg and ECB = EVB − Eg . Herein, the X values of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were 5.56 and 5.96 eV, respectively [19,36]. As a result, the ECB and EVB values of CeO2 were respectively −0.41 and 2.53 eV, and the ECB and EVB values of Zn3V2O8 were separately −0.04 and 2.96 eV in the light of the aforementioned formulas. In addition, the ECB and EVB values of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were further determined by the Mott-Schottky measurement [7,57]. From Fig. S10, it was discovered that both CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 depicted the positive slope, illustrating that they were n-type semiconductors [58]. Meanwhile, the flat band potentials (Efb ) of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were respectively estimated to be −0.52 and −0.15 V (Fig. S10a and S10b). Considering the fact that the ECB of n-type semiconductor is approximately lower about 0.1 V than its Efb
[4,57,59,60], thus the ECB of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were separately calculated to be −0.62 and −0.25 V vs. Ag/AgCl, and the corresponding normal hydrogen electrode potentials were −0.42 and −0.05 V vs. NHE [4,61,62], respectively. The EVB values of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were separately calculated to be 2.52 and 2.95 eV by the following equation: EVB = ECB + Eg , which were basically in agreement with the results of the above-mentioned empirical formulas. Moreover, the EVB values of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were further verified by valence band (VB) XPS analysis, and the results were presented in Fig. S8. It could be seen that the VB values of CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were respectively estimated to be 2.48 eV (Fig. S11a) and 2.89 eV (Fig. S11b) through extrapolating the corresponding VB-XPS curves, which was roughly consistent with the results of the aforementioned empirical formulas and Mott-Schottky measurement.

In order to acquire insight into the relationship between the recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers and photocatalytic activity, photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and photoelectrochemical measurements were further carried out. Fig. 8 a illustrated the PL spectra of the as-prepared Zn3V2O8 and CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrids were measured at excitation wavelength of 325 nm. It was clear to see that all the samples display broad luminescence peak located at around 550 nm. Interestingly, the intensity of the emission peak significantly weakened after the introduction of CeO2, indicating that the recombination rate of charge in the composite was effectively suppressed. Especially, it was observed that the CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid exhibited the lowest intensity of the emission peak, demonstrating that a higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which was beneficial to improving photocatalytic performance. It was further verified by the following the results of photocurrent, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectroscopy analysis. It could be seen from Fig. 8b that the photocurrent density of CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 was higher than that of pure CeO2 and Zn3V2O8, revealing that the CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 heterojunction possessed the faster transfer rate of charge. Moreover, from Fig. 8c, it could be observed that the CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid owned the smaller arc radius compared with pure CeO2 and Zn3V2O8, indicating that the resistance of carriers transfer was minimized after the introduction of CeO2 on the surface of Zn3V2O8, which was more favourable for charge transfer As depicted in Fig. 8d, it was clear to see that the prepared CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid displayed the prolonged life time decay (22.1 ns) compared to pure CeO2 (18.0 ns) and Zn3V2O8 (19.4 ns), elucidating that an effective electron transport and separation process was achieved in the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid [63,64]. In short, it could be concluded that the rapid separation efficiency of carriers was one of the main reason for highly improved photocatalytic performance of the prepared CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid. The rapid separation of carriers was ascribed to the construction of the Z-scheme heterojunction between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8.

To identify the active radicals produced in the photocatalytic process, a series of active species capture experiments were carried out. Herein, 4-hydroxy-2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidinyloxy (TEMPOL), tertiary butanol (TBA) and triethanolamine (TEOA) were utilized to selectively eliminate superoxide (
O 2 -
) and hydroxyl (
OH) radicals, and holes (h+), respectively [40,65,66]. As displayed in Fig. 9 a, it was observed that the photocatalytic degradation efficiency was significantly inhibited when TEMPOL or TEOA were added to the reaction system, demonstrating that
O 2 -
and h+ were both the pivotal active species generated in the degradation process. Furthermore, it was clear to see that the elimination rate of TC greatly restrained when the TC solution was purged by N2, suggesting that superoxide radicals played a significant role in the elimination of TC. On the contrary, it was observed that the addition of TBA had no obvious inhibitory influence on the degradation of TC, implying that
OH was not important active species in the degradation process. The electron spin resonance (ESR) technology was employed to further support evidence for above results. From Fig. S12, it was clear to see that there were no unambiguous signals of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-
O 2 -
for all obtained samples were detected under dark condition. Noticeably, it was found that the unambiguous characteristic peaks of DMPO-
OH could be observed for pure CeO2, Zn3V2O8 and CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid after 5 min visible light irradiation (Fig. S13a), suggesting that hydroxyl radicals were produced in the degradation process [67,68], because the VB values of both CeO2 (2.53 eV) and Zn3V2O8 (2.96 eV) were more positive to the standard oxidation potential of H2O/
OH (2.38 eV) [68]. Besides, it was observed that the obvious characteristic peaks of DMPO-
O 2 -
could be observed for both CeO2 and CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid after 5 min visible light irradiation (Fig. 9b), confirming that superoxide radicals were produced in the degradation process [67,68], because the CB value (−0.41 eV) of CeO2 was more negative to the standard reduction potential (−0.33 eV) of O2/
O 2 -
[68]. Interestingly, there were no unambiguous signals of DMPO-
O 2 -
were observed for pure Zn3V2O8 (Fig. 9b), indicating that no
O 2 -
was generated by Zn3V2O8 in the degradation process, since the CB value (−0.04 eV) of Zn3V2O8 was less negative to the standard reduction potential (−0.33 eV) of O2/
O 2 -
. Dramatically, it was clear to see that the intensities of DMPO-
OH signals for CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 were stronger than those of pure CeO2 and Zn3V2O8, and the intensities of DMPO-
O 2 -
signals for CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 were greatly larger than that of single CeO2 (Fig. 9b and S13a), suggesting that more hydroxyl and superoxide radicals were produced by CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8, which was probably ascribed to the construction of unique CeO2/Zn3V2O8 Z-scheme heterojunction improved the separation of charge carriers and favored the generation of superoxide and hydroxyl radicals. More importantly, it was observed that the intensities of DMPO-
O 2 -
signals for CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 were much stronger than those of DMPO-
OH signals (Fig. S13b), illustrating that the superoxide radicals probably played a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation of TC under visible light irradiation, which was agreement with the results of active species trapping experiments. Similar results were also obtained by the BiOCl@CeO2 composite [36]. In short, these findings clearly confirming that the generated
O 2 -
and h+ were both essential for the degradation of TC over the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid under visible light irradiation.

To further elucidate the photocatalytic removal mechanism of TC, the degradation products of TC were confirmed by high-performance liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS). As could be seen from Fig. S14, the mass peak at m/z = 445 corresponded to protonated TC, and thirteen main mass peaks at m/z = 476, 461, 459, 432, 416, 388, 359, 349, 344, 305, 300, 278 and 261 belonged to the intermediates. Furthermore, there were three possible degradation pathways of TC over CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 were illustrated in Fig. S15. For the pathway I, the intermediate A (m/z = 432) was generated by TC hydroxylation and N-dealkylation, and then the intermediates including B (m/z = 388), C (m/z = 349), D (m/z = 305) and E (m/z = 261) were further formed via the deamidization, dealkylation, deamination, dehydroxylation and ring-opening reactions [69,70]. In terms of pathway Ⅲ, the intermediate F (m/z = 416) was produced by TC dealkylation, and then the intermediates including G (m/z = 359) and H (m/z = 278) were also generated via the hydroxylation, ring-opening and deamination processes [71,72]. In terms of pathway Ⅲ, the intermediate I (m/z = 476) was generated by TC hydroxylation, and then the intermediates including J (m/z = 459), K (m/z = 344) and L (m/z = 300) were further formed via the dehydroxylation, N-dealkylation, ring-opening and decarbonylation processes [70]. Finally, all the intermediates could be further degraded into H2O, CO2 and some other small intermediates.

Combined with the aforementioned discussion, a reasonable photocatalytic mechanism of the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid for the degradation TC under visible light irradiation was tentatively proposed and illustrated in Fig. 10 . Intuitively, there were two types of charge migration routes between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 due to their overlapped band potentials, namely, conventional type II and Z-scheme heterojunction [73–75]. First of all, under visible light illumination, CeO2 and Zn3V2O8 were both excited to generate the photoinduced electrons and holes on the corresponding CB and VB due to their appropriate band gaps. If the charge migration pathway followed the traditional type II heterojunction (Fig. 10a), the photoexcited electrons in the CB of CeO2 could easily shift to the CB of Zn3V2O8, because the CB value (−0.41 eV) of CeO2 was lower than that (−0.04 eV) of Zn3V2O8, and the photoexcited holes in the VB of Zn3V2O8 would transfer to the VB of CeO2 since the VB value (2.96 eV) of Zn3V2O8 was more positive than that (2.53 eV) of CeO2. In this way, the photoexcited carriers were spatially separated. Unfortunately, at this condition,
O 2 -
was not able to be generated by the adsorptive O2 reacted with the accumulated electrons in the CB of Zn3V2O8, since the CB value (−0.04 eV) of Zn3V2O8 was not negative than the standard reduction potential (−0.33 eV) of O2/
O 2 -
[68,75]. It was not consistent with the results of active species trapping experiments and ESR measurement, so the traditional type II heterojunction mechanism was not appropriate to explain the degradation mechanism over the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid. Hence, the Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism was more suitable to elucidate the enhanced photocatalytic mechanism. As presented in Fig. 10b, the photoexcited electrons in the CB of the Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers would be transferred to the junction interface and would combine with the photoinduced holes in the VB of the CeO2 nanoparticles. As a result, the strong reducibility of photoinduced electrons in the CB of the CeO2 nanoparticles and the high oxidizability of photoexcited holes in the VB of the Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers were retained. Subsequently, the accumulated in the CB of CeO2 could be efficiently trapped by the surrounding adsorbed O2 to form
O 2 -
, which was believed to be an important active radical to oxidize TC, since the CB potential (−0.41 eV) of CeO2 was lower than that of O2/
O 2 -
(−0.33 eV). The production of superoxide radicals were verified by the ESR test. Meanwhile, the remained holes in VB of Zn3V2O8 could directly eliminate TC to degradation products. Apparently, both
O 2 -
and h+ could be generated by the constructed Z-scheme heterojunction, which was well consistent with the results of active species trapping experiments. More importantly, this Z-scheme heterojunction could effectively consume relatively useless photogenerated electrons (from the CB of Zn3V2O8) and holes (from the VB of CeO2), and preserve useful photogenerated electrons (from the CB of CeO2) and holes (from the VB of Zn3V2O8). As a consequence, this Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism optimized the transfer path of photogenerated electrons and holes, boosted the separation efficiency of photoexcited charge carriers, endowing CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid with higher oxidation and reduction capabilities, consequently greatly promoting the degradation of TC under visible light illumination. Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation of TC over the resulting CeO2/Zn3V2O8 photocatalyst under visible light illumination could be described as following equations:

In conclusion, a visible light responsive hierarchical zero-dimensional/ three-dimensional (0D/3D) CeO2/Zn3V2O8 Z-scheme heterojunction hybrid was successfully constructed through a facile mixing process. It was worth noting that 0D CeO2 nanoparticles decorated on the surface of 3D Zn3V2O8 nanoflowers owned multiple functions, including providing high-efficiency charge migration channel, more catalytic active sites and promoting the migration rate of photogenerated charge carriers. As expected, the prepared CeO2/Zn3V2O8 hybrid illustrated the enhanced photocatalytic performance for TC degradation under visible light illumination. Especially, it was observed that the optimized CeO2(40 wt%)/Zn3V2O8 hybrid showed the highest removal rate of TC with 1.13 × 10−2 min−1, which was approximately 8.1 and 3.8 folds as high as bare CeO2 (0.14 × 10−2 min−1) and Zn3V2O8 (0.30 × 10−2 min−1), respectively. This enhancement was mainly be ascribed to the formation of unique 0D/3D CeO2/Zn3V2O8 Z-scheme heterojunction hybrid between CeO2 and Zn3V2O8, which not only greatly accelerated the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers, but also maintained the high reduction capacity of the photoexcited electrons in CeO2 and strong oxidation ability of photoinduced holes in Zn3V2O8. Additionally, the photogenerated holes and superoxide radicals were considered as dominate active species via active species capture experiments and electron spin resonance spectra, and the possible degradation pathways of TC and the reasonable degradation mechanism including migration and transport behaviors of charge carriers and generation processes of reactive species were discussed in depth. This investigation offers a new idea for the establishment and fabrication of high-activity Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalysts for energy conversion and environmental remediation.

Jiayi Chen: Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Software. Xiaoting Chen: Investigation, Resources, Data curation. Xiaomei Ning: Formal analysis, Software, Writing - review & editing. Liang Zhan: Visualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xiaosong Zhou: Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
